questions.question: A parliamentary system of government is one in which
questions.option_1: All political parties in the parliament are represented in the government
questions.option_2: The government is responsible to the Parliament and can be removed by it
questions.option_3: The government is elected by the people and can be removed by them
questions.option_4: The government is chosen by the parliament but cannot be removed by it before the completion of a fixed term
questions.solution:
Parliamentary System of Government

A Parliamentary System is a system of democratic governance where the executive (the government) derives its democratic legitimacy from its ability to command the confidence of the legislature (the parliament). It is also held accountable to that legislature.

  • Accountability: The executive is collectively responsible to the Parliament, specifically the lower house (Lok Sabha in India).
  • Removal: The government stays in power only as long as it enjoys the majority support in the parliament. If a No-Confidence Motion is passed, the government is obliged to resign.
  • Fusion of Powers: Unlike the Presidential system where the executive and legislature are separate, in a parliamentary system, the executive is a part of the legislature.

questions.question: In the context of India which one of the following is the characteristic appropriate for Bureaucracy?
questions.option_1: An agency for widening the scope of parliamentary democracy
questions.option_2: An agency for strengthening the structure of federalism
questions.option_3: An agency for facilitating political stability and economic growth
questions.option_4: An agency for the implementation of public policy
questions.solution:
Role of Bureaucracy

While bureaucracy helps in various administrative functions, its fundamental and defining characteristic in a democratic setup like India is the implementation of public policy.

  • Policy Execution: While the political executive (Ministers) frames policies, the permanent executive (Bureaucrats) is responsible for translating these policies into action on the ground.
  • Anonymity and Neutrality: Bureaucrats are expected to implement the policies of the government of the day regardless of their personal political leanings.
  • Rule of Law: They ensure that administration is carried out according to the established laws and regulations.

questions.question: Democracy's superior virtue lies in the fact that it calls into activity
questions.option_1: The intelligence and character of ordinary men and women.
questions.option_2: The methods for strengthening executive leadership
questions.option_3: A superior individual with dynamism and vision.
questions.option_4: A band of dedicated party workers.
questions.solution:
Virtue of Democracy

Democracy is often considered superior not because it guarantees the most efficient government, but because of its impact on the human personality and civic engagement.

  • Civic Participation: It encourages ordinary citizens to take an interest in public affairs, thereby developing their intellectual and moral faculties.
  • Character Building: By giving individuals a stake in the governance process, it fosters a sense of responsibility, self-reliance, and dignity.
  • Inclusivity: It relies on the collective wisdom of the masses rather than the 'vision' of a single individual or a small elite group.

questions.question: The main advantage of the parliamentary form of government is that
questions.option_1: the executive and legislature work independently
questions.option_2: it provides continuity of policy and is more efficient.
questions.option_3: the executive remains responsible to the legislature
questions.option_4: the head of the government cannot be changed without election.
questions.solution:
Advantage of Parliamentary Form

The hallmark of the parliamentary form of government is the continuous accountability of the executive to the legislature.

FeatureAdvantage
ResponsibilityThe executive is answerable for its actions and policies to the parliament.
Prevents DespotismSince the executive is accountable, it prevents the concentration of power in a single individual.
CoordinationHarmonious cooperation between the legislature and executive as they are not strictly separated.

questions.question: Out of the following statements, choose the one that brings out the principle underlying the Cabinet form of Government:
questions.option_1: An arrangement for minimizing the criticism against the Government whose responsibilities are complex and hard to carry out to the satisfaction of all.
questions.option_2: A mechanism for speeding up the activities of the Government whose responsibilities are increasing day by day.
questions.option_3: A mechanism of parliamentary democracy for ensuring collective responsibility of the Government to the people.
questions.option_4: A device for strengthening the hands of the head of the Government whose hold over the people is in a state of decline.
questions.solution:
Cabinet Form of Government

The principle underlying the Cabinet form of government (often called the Westminster model) is Collective Responsibility.

  • Unity: The Council of Ministers acts as a single unit. Decisions made in Cabinet meetings are binding on all ministers.
  • Responsibility to the People: Since the Cabinet is responsible to the Parliament (the representative body of the people), it is the primary mechanism ensuring the government remains responsive to public will.
  • Resignation: If the Lok Sabha passes a vote of no-confidence against the Council of Ministers, the entire cabinet must resign, illustrating the principle of 'swimming or sinking together'.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not a feature of Indian federalism?
questions.option_1: There is an independent judiciary in India.
questions.option_2: Powers have been clearly divided between the Centre and the States.
questions.option_3: The federating units have been given unequal representation in the Rajya Sabha.
questions.option_4: It is the result of an agreement among the federating units.
questions.solution:
Features of Indian Federalism

Indian federalism is unique and is often described as 'Quasi-federal'. One of its distinct features is that it was not formed by an agreement among the states.

  • Not a Compact: Unlike the USA, where independent states came together to form a federation, the Indian federation is an indestructible union of destructible states. States do not have the right to secede.
  • Independent Judiciary: A core federal feature to settle disputes between Center and States.
  • Division of Powers: Clearly defined through the Seventh Schedule (Union, State, and Concurrent Lists).
  • Unequal Representation: Seats in the Rajya Sabha are allotted based on population, not equality of states (unlike the US Senate).

questions.question: There is a Parliamentary System of Government in India because the
questions.option_1: Lok Sabha is elected directly by the people
questions.option_4: Council of Ministers is responsible to the Lok Sabha
questions.solution:
Parliamentary System in India

The definitive reason India is classified as a Parliamentary system is provided under Article 75(3) of the Constitution.

The Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the House of the People (Lok Sabha).

This institutional link between the executive and the legislature ensures that the executive is not independent of the legislative branch but is sustained by it.

questions.question: The Parliament can make any law for whole or any part of India for implementing international treaties
questions.option_1: With the consent of all the States
questions.option_2: With the consent of the majority of States
questions.option_3: With the consent of the States concerned
questions.option_4: Without the consent of any State
questions.solution:
International Treaties and Parliament

According to Article 253 of the Constitution, the Parliament has the power to make any law for the whole or any part of the territory of India for implementing any treaty, agreement, or convention with any other country.

  • Central Supremacy: This can be done without the consent of any state.
  • Uniformity: This provision ensures that the Union government can fulfill its international obligations effectively without being hindered by the distribution of legislative powers in the Seventh Schedule.

questions.question:
In the context of India, which of the following principles is/are implied institutionally in the parliamentary government?

  1. Members of the Cabinet are Members of the Parliament.
  2. Ministers hold the office till they enjoy confidence in the Parliament.
  3. The Cabinet is headed by the Head of the State.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
questions.solution:
Institutional Principles of Parliamentary Government

In India, the parliamentary system implies several institutional arrangements:

  1. Membership: A minister must be a member of the Parliament (either Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha). If a non-member is appointed, they must get elected within six months.
  2. Confidence: The executive (Council of Ministers) holds office only as long as it enjoys the 'confidence' (majority) of the Lok Sabha.
  3. Head of State: The President is the nominal (titular) Head of the State, whereas the Prime Minister is the head of the Cabinet/Government. Therefore, the statement that the Cabinet is headed by the Head of the State is incorrect.

questions.question:
With reference to Union Government, consider the following statements:

  1. The Constitution of India provides that all Cabinet Ministers shall be compulsorily the sitting members of Lok Sabha only
  2. The Union Cabinet Secretariat operates under the direction of the Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Union Government Structure

The statements provided are incorrect based on the following constitutional and administrative facts:

  • Statement 1: Ministers can be from either the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha. There is no requirement for them to be members of the Lok Sabha only.
  • Statement 2: The Cabinet Secretariat operates directly under the Prime Minister. The administrative head of the Secretariat is the Cabinet Secretary, who is also the ex-officio Chairman of the Civil Services Board.

questions.question: The Indian parliamentary system is different from the British parliamentary system in that India has
questions.option_1: Both a real and a nominal executive
questions.solution:
India vs. Britain: Parliamentary Differences

While India adopted the British model, there are fundamental differences:

FeatureBritish SystemIndian System
SovereigntyParliament is Supreme/Sovereign.Constitution is Supreme; Parliament's power is limited.
Head of StateHereditary Monarchy.Elected Head (Republic).
Judicial ReviewLimited/Non-existent against Acts of Parliament.Strong Judicial Review; Courts can declare laws unconstitutional.
Prime MinisterMust be from the Lower House.Can be from either House of Parliament.
